items.title: Hook-creation flow can ship fragile bash-regex security restrictions without catching common bypasses
items.desc:
## Description

When asked to create a Bash `PreToolUse`/`PermissionRequest` hook restricting `rm -r`/`rm -rf` to specific paths, Claude implemented it as a `grep -qE '^rm -r...'` bash one-liner and declared it tested/working after checking only the happy-path case (`rm -rf /allowed/path/...`).

Only after the user explicitly asked "what about rm -rf?" as a follow-up did Claude actually stress-test the hook — at which point it turned out the regex silently failed to catch:

- `rm -Rf`, `rm -fr`,...

items.title: fix(security): validate port range in suspicious-network scanner rule
items.desc:
## What Problem This Solves

The `suspicious-network` scanner rule uses `Number.parseInt` on a regex capture group to extract a port number, then checks `STANDARD_PORTS.has(port)`. If `parseInt` returns `NaN` (e.g. from unexpected regex match content), `STANDARD_PORTS.has(NaN)` returns `false`, so the finding is reported with a `NaN` port — a false positive with a meaningless value. Negative ports and values exceeding 65535 are also not valid TCP/UDP ports but would bypass the standard-port allo...

## Why This Change Was Made

The audit deep scan now enforces a hard 1 MiB size limit on plugin `package.json` reads, using the existing `statRegularFile` / `readRegularFile` helper...

items.title: Security: COMMAND_INJECTION in commands.py:974 (subprocess shell=True)
items.desc:
**Vulnerability:** COMMAND_INJECTION
**File:** aider/commands.py:974
**Severity:** Critical

**Vulnerable code:**
subprocess.run("git " + args, shell=True)

**Why it's dangerous:**
User-controlled git args passed to shell=True
→ /git status; cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa possible
→ SSH key theft

**Fix:**
subprocess.run(["git"] + args.split(), shell=False)

**How I found it:**
pip install aina-scan
aina-vibeguard scan .

`SocketTransport.sendFrame` (the host-side `ncl` transport) has no request timeout and no bound on the response buffer. The server side has the matching gaps.

items.title: Security: Docker Compose disables core container confinement
items.desc:
## Security hardening issue

The default Docker Compose configuration grants `SYS_ADMIN` and disables both AppArmor and seccomp:

```yaml
cap_add:
  - SYS_ADMIN
security_opt:
  - apparmor=unconfined
  - seccomp=unconfined
```

This is in `docker-compose.yml:1-10`. Nanobot also runs shell commands and external tools, so weakening container confinement materially increases breakout impact.

## Suggested fix

Avoid `SYS_ADMIN` unless a narrowly scoped sandbox requires it, retain the default seccomp...

Config files (containing API keys) and session files (containing conversation history) are created with default umask permissions (`0o644` / `rw-r--r--`), allowing other local users on shared systems to read sensitive data.
